text:
Overview of the TREC 2025 Retrieval Augmented
Generation (RAG) Track

Shivani Upadhyay1, Nandan Thakur1, Ronak Pradeep1,
Nick Craswell2, Daniel Campos3, Jimmy Lin1

1 University of Waterloo
2 Microsoft
3 Zipf AI

arXiv:2603.09891v1 [cs.IR] 10 Mar 2026

Abstract

The second edition of the TREC Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) Track
advances research on systems that integrate retrieval and generation to address
complex, real-world information needs. Building on the foundation of the inaugural
2024 track, this year’s challenge introduces long, multi-sentence narrative queries
to better reflect the deep search task with the growing demand for reasoning-driven
responses. Participants are tasked with designing pipelines that combine retrieval
and generation while ensuring transparency and factual grounding. The track
leverages the MS MARCO V2.1 corpus and employs a multi-layered evaluation
framework encompassing relevance assessment, response completeness, attribution
verification, and agreement analysis. By emphasizing multi-faceted narratives and
attribution-rich answers from over 150 submissions this year, the TREC 2025 RAG
Track aims to foster innovation in creating trustworthy, context-aware systems for
retrieval augmented generation. Track website:

1
Introduction

This paper provides an overview of the TREC 2025 Retrieval Augment Generation (RAG) Track. The
second edition of the Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) Track builds on the foundation laid by
the inaugural TREC 2024 RAG Track [4, 9, 5, 8], pushing research further into systems that integrate
retrieval and generation for complex, real-world information needs. We harness the institutional
knowledge and resources provided by the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) via
the Text Retrieval Conference (TREC) to tackle these challenges. Now in its 34th year, TREC has
led the way in many aspects of evaluation in information retrieval (IR), natural language processing
(NLP), and beyond, producing many innovations that the community (both researchers as well as
practitioners) take for granted today. Building upon last year’s evaluation strategy at the TREC 2024 RAG Track, a key change this year is
moving from short, keyword-style queries to multi-sentence, long and complex narratives that mimic
deep search scenarios. This shift reflects the growing demand for RAG and agentic systems (or
agents) capable of nuanced interpretation, broader evidence coverage, and reasoning-driven responses. Participants are challenged to design pipelines that not only combine retrieval and generation but also
maintain transparency and factual grounding in their outputs. These changes aim to foster innovation
in creating systems that can handle intricate queries and deliver well-supported, context-rich answers. To support these advancements, the track continues to use the MS MARCO V2.1 document corpus
constructed last year at the TREC 2024 RAG Track, which contains segmentation and document
deduplication, useful for assessing retrieval and generation tasks in our track. The corpus ensures
a diverse document representation, enabling participants to build systems that can retrieve relevant
evidence and generate responses grounded in verifiable and reliable sources. By introducing narratives
and emphasizing attribution, the TREC 2025 RAG Track encourages approaches that go beyond

Query: I’m interested in sports’ societal impact, particularly concerning athlete compensation, inclusion, cultural
influence, and the business side. I also want to understand how evolving equipment, training, and mindset shape
both athletes and the popularity of different sports. Answer: Sports exert profound societal impacts through athlete compensation, inclusion, cultural dynamics, and
commercialization, while evolving equipment, training, and mindsets reshape athletic performance and sport
popularity. Athlete compensation disparities persist, with female athletes and college athletes receiving inequitable
pay compared to their male and professional counterparts, raising ethical questions about just compensation and the
commodification of athletes [0]. College athletes, despite generating significant revenue, often lack direct salaries,
though debates persist over whether scholarships suffice or if additional compensation is warranted [0]. Inclusion
remains a challenge, as racial and gender disparities persist in leadership roles and access to opportunities. For
example, Black women are underrepresented in sports like field hockey and golf, while Title IX has increased
female participation but not fully addressed coaching diversity. Socioeconomic barriers further limit access to elite
sports, as expensive equipment and coaching exclude lower-income individuals [2]. Culturally, sports reflect and
reinforce societal values, serving as a microcosm of racial and gender dynamics. They also foster unity, transcending
social and political divides, while commercialization ties sports to consumerism, with sponsorships prioritizing
brand visibility over equitable access [3]. The business side of sports, driven by media and corporate interests,
amplifies revenue but risks prioritizing profit over athlete welfare, as seen in debates over performance-enhancing
drugs and gambling [6]. Evolving equipment and training methods, such as advanced analytics and technology,
enhance performance but raise ethical concerns about fairness and health risks [5]. Mental and physical training
paradigms, including discipline and resilience, influence athlete success and public perceptions of sports [4]. Meanwhile, shifting societal values—like prioritizing health and inclusivity—reshape popular sports, with pleasure
and participation sports gaining traction as alternatives to high-stakes power sports [5]. These interwoven factors
highlight sports’ dual role as both a reflection of societal progress and a catalyst for change, though persistent
inequities and commercial pressures underscore ongoing challenges [1]. Table 1: Sample answer from the TREC 2025 RAG Track for narrative 14 with citations highlighted.

surface-level matching, requiring deeper reasoning and synthesis of information across multiple
documents. This year, the track employs a multi-layered evaluation workflow to ensure reliability
and depth:

1. Relevance Assessment: Human assessors judge document relevance using sub-narratives, as-
signing relevance scores between 0 (irrelevant) and 4 (perfectly relevant) based on the number of
sub-narratives being answered in the given document.

2. Response Evaluation: For relevant documents, key information units (“nuggets”) are extracted
and mapped to sub-narratives to measure content coverage and answer completeness.

3. Support Evaluation: Each sentence in a generated answer is checked against its cited document
to verify factual grounding.

4. Agreement Analysis: System labels are compared with human judgments using metrics such as
Cohen’s κ to assess alignment beyond chance.

and mapped to sub-narratives to measure content coverage and answer completeness.

3. Support Evaluation: Each sentence in a generated answer is checked against its cited document
to verify factual grounding.

4. Agreement Analysis: System labels are compared with human judgments using metrics such as
Cohen’s κ to assess alignment beyond chance. This layered approach combines manual and automated methods for assessment, enabling a fine-
grained evaluation of retrieval quality, attribution accuracy, and response completeness. By emphasiz-
ing the narrative-driven queries and attribution-rich answers, the TREC 2025 RAG Track aims to
push the boundaries of retrieval-augmented generation research, fostering innovations that improve
reasoning, coverage, and trustworthiness in AI-driven systems.

2
Task Setup

The TREC 2025 RAG track comprised four interconnected tasks: (1) Retrieval (R), (2) Augmented
Generation (AG), (3) Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG), and (4) Relevance Judgment (RJ) (a
new task this year); all built on the MS MARCO v2.1 segmented document collection [4], which
addressed deduplication and provided fine-grained segmentation to support robust retrieval and
grounded generation. Participants were given 105 queries (called narratives in TREC parlance); their
ultimate task was to return, for the AG and RAG tasks, well-formed answers for each narrative (up to
a maximum of 400 words). The Retrieval (R) task could be viewed as an intermediate product in a
full RAG pipeline. Furthermore, the Relevance Judgment (RJ) task, required participants to provide a
set of relevance judgments. Throughout this paper, the narrative 14: “I’m interested in sports’ societal impact, particularly
concerning athlete compensation, inclusion, cultural influence, and the business side. I also want to

• Retrieval (R): In the Retrieval (R) task, participants are given a set of narratives (each 2–3
sentences in JSONL format with narrative id and title) and must return a TREC-style ranked list

Actual submissions to the TREC 2025 RAG Track
were in the form of structured JSON, wherein each answer sentence is explicitly linked to citations of
documents within the corpus that (purportedly) supported the assertions made in the sentence [4]. Here is an overview of the four tasks conducted at the TREC 2025 RAG Track:

• Retrieval (R): In the Retrieval (R) task, participants are given a set of narratives (each 2–3
sentences in JSONL format with narrative id and title) and must return a TREC-style ranked list
of the top-100 segment IDs from the MS MARCO V2.1 deduped segment collection [4]. Unlike
previous MS MARCO data, where passages were selected to be short human-readable answers, the
V2.1 passages were constructed using a sliding window approach over full documents to generate
more “natural” content. These passages form segments (sometimes called “chunks”) suitable for
RAG.

• Augmented Generation (AG): The AG task isolates the generation component by fixing retrieval:
participants receive a top-k ranked list of segments (produced by the baseline retrieval pipeline)
and must generate an answer of ≤400 words, broken into sentences, with sentence-level citations
to supporting segment IDs from the provided top-k ranked list.

• Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG): The RAG task evaluates end-to-end systems; partici-
pants bring their own retrievers, rerankers, generators, and pipeline, but must map all evidence to
MS MARCO V2.1 segment IDs for evaluation.

• Relevance Judgment (RJ): The Relevance Judgment (RJ) task determines how well individual
documents address the information need expressed in a narrative. Each document is scored on a 0–4
scale, where 0 indicates the document is not relevant and 4 indicates comprehensive coverage of the
narrative. This task is designed to capture varying degrees of relevance for complex, multi-sentence
queries, ensuring that retrieval systems are evaluated on their ability to return documents that
meaningfully contribute to answering the full narrative. Reference Ranked Lists. Reference ranked lists of top-k segments were provided for those who
did not wish to perform retrieval on the document collection themselves. This represented the input
to the AG task, where participants could focus on the “augmented generation” part of RAG. The
top documents for each narrative were provided based on an effective multi-stage ranking pipeline,
described below:

The provided ranked lists included a combination of first-stage retrieval and advanced reranking
strategies. For first-stage retrieval, reciprocal rank fusion (RRF) across two high-performing models:
SPLADE-v3 [1] and Snowflake’s Arctic-Embed-L [3] (each retrieving the top 1,000 documents) was
employed. This hybrid approach leveraged the complementary strengths of sparse and dense retrieval,
as RRF has consistently demonstrated improved retrieval effectiveness across multiple evaluation
benchmarks in the RAG 2024 track [4]. Following retrieval, a reranking pipeline was deployed to refine the candidate lists. RankLLM [6], a
neural reranker was applied to process the fused retrieval results and improve narrative-document
relevance. Following this, RankQwen3-32B, a reranker operating on the top 1,000 candidates from
the previous stage, produced a reordered list, from which the top 100 documents were selected. This

cascading reranking strategy ensured that the final ranked lists reflected both lexical and semantic
relevance, providing participants with highly optimized results. Narrative Generation. The TREC 2025 RAG Track required narrative-style queries; however,
these were not readily available, and it was cumbersome to generate them manually using human
annotators. Therefore, the automated narrative generation process transformed clusters of related
queries into coherent, multi-sentence narratives that simulated real-world research needs. For example,
a cluster of queries such as “Are professional athletes overpaid?” and “How does professional sports
influence culture?” was expanded into a narrative like: “I’m interested in sports’ societal impact,
particularly concerning athlete compensation, inclusion, cultural influence, and the business side. I
also want to understand how evolving equipment, training, and mindset shape both athletes and the
popularity of different sports.” Figure 1 presents an overview of the overall process.

semi-automated. The first phase of this process utilized GPT-4.1 nano, which was provided with the narrative and its
corresponding query list (used to generate the narrative) and was instructed to generate an exhaustive
collection of candidate sub-narratives (see Figure 2). Each prompt produced a JSON-formatted
list of dictionary objects, where each object included a text field describing the sub-narrative and
an importance field denoting its relevance to the narrative (vital or okay). In the second phase,
both the original narrative and the preliminary sub-narrative list obtained from the preceding step
are supplied to GPT-4.1, which generated a refined and more coherent set of sub-narratives while
adhering to the same JSON output structure (see Figure 3). Finally, each narrative and sub-narrative
pair was systematically reviewed and analyzed by human annotators. When necessary, the resulting
sub-narrative lists were subsequently post-edited to ensure completeness, coherence, and alignment
with the intended narrative scope. Figure 4 illustrates an example of the narrative and sub-narrative

3
Evaluation

3.1
Relevance Assessment
Relevance assessment at the TREC 2025 RAG Track focuses on determining how well retrieved
documents satisfy complex, multi-faceted information needs expressed through narratives. These
narratives often encompass diverse aspects, making direct judgment on the entire narrative challenging. To address this complexity, the process begins by decomposing each narrative into a set of sub-
narratives, which represent simpler and more focused components of the original information need. This decomposition enables assessors to evaluate relevance in a structured and consistent manner,
reducing ambiguity and improving reliability across judgments. The original narrative is broken down into distinct sub-narratives that capture specific thematic
dimensions. For example, the broad narrative about sports and its societal impact is decomposed
into focused areas such as athlete compensation issues, sports and cultural influence, role of athlete
mindset changes, societal impact of sports, and business influences in sports.

This decomposition enables assessors to evaluate relevance in a structured and consistent manner,
reducing ambiguity and improving reliability across judgments. The original narrative is broken down into distinct sub-narratives that capture specific thematic
dimensions. For example, the broad narrative about sports and its societal impact is decomposed
into focused areas such as athlete compensation issues, sports and cultural influence, role of athlete
mindset changes, societal impact of sports, and business influences in sports. These sub-narratives
make it easier to analyze and address different aspects of the overarching narrative. This structured
approach ensures that complex narratives are organized into manageable, well-defined components
for deeper exploration. Once sub-narratives are established, assessors assign relevance labels to segments using a five-point
scale based on coverage: 0 (Irrelevant) for segments unrelated to the narrative, 1 (Related) for
segments offering general but non-specific information, 2 (Relevant) for content addressing one
sub-narrative, 3 (Highly Relevant) for segments covering two or three sub-narratives, and 4 (Perfectly
Relevant) for those addressing four or more sub-narratives. This approach simplifies the evaluation
of complex narratives, ensures alignment between assessors, and provides a robust foundation for
subsequent stages such as nugget extraction and attribution. By focusing on sub-narrative coverage
rather than the entire narrative, the relevance assessment process enhances interpretability and fairness
in evaluating retrieval and generation systems [7]. Table 2 compares the relevance assessment labels

3.2
Response Evaluation

Response evaluation in TREC 2025 RAG follows a two-step process to measure both the quality and
coverage of system outputs.

• Step 1: Nugget Generation and Assignment involves identifying and classifying key information
units (nuggets) from relevant documents and determining their presence in system responses.

• Step 2: Sub-Narrative Mapping aligns these nuggets with sub-narratives to assess how compre-
hensively the response addresses the intended information need. Table 3 (left) illustrates the nugget
to sub-narrative example for the sports narrative. For the assignment, strict vital recall is used, as it is found to be best suited for response evaluation in
the TREC RAG 2024 Track [5]. In particular, this metric calculates recall over all vital nuggets that
are fully supported by the response. Table 3 (right) presents the assignment for the sports example. The sub-narrative Athlete compensation issues is marked as covered since at least one of the mapped
nuggets is fully supported by the response used for this example.

For the assignment, strict vital recall is used, as it is found to be best suited for response evaluation in
the TREC RAG 2024 Track [5]. In particular, this metric calculates recall over all vital nuggets that
are fully supported by the response. Table 3 (right) presents the assignment for the sports example. The sub-narrative Athlete compensation issues is marked as covered since at least one of the mapped
nuggets is fully supported by the response used for this example. AutoNuggets + AutoAssign. In this setting, both steps are fully automated by LLMs. Nugget gen-
eration begins by extracting concise, semantically meaningful information units from all documents
marked as related or higher (relevance label ≥1). This is implemented using AutoNuggetizer [5], a
system that integrates multiple LLMs, including Gemini 2.5 Pro, GPT-4.1, Qwen3 Thinking 32B,
and GPT-OSS 120B. Each model independently produces candidate nuggets, which are then merged
at last using GPT-4.1 to ensure consistency and eliminate redundancy. AutoNuggetizer also classifies nuggets into two categories: vital and okay. A vital nugget represents
indispensable information required to fully answer the narrative, while an okay nugget adds helpful
but non-essential details. Using these nuggets, AutoNuggetizer assigns nugget-level support labels
to system responses. AutoNuggetizer adopts a listwise approach for nugget assignment, where the
LLM (here GPT-4.1) analyzes an answer and determines whether each nugget is fully supported (Full
Support), partially supported (Partial Support), or not supported (No Support) by the answer. The second step, sub-narrative mapping, uses GPT-4.1 to align each nugget with its corresponding
sub-narrative. If a nugget does not fit any existing sub-narrative, a new sub-narrative is created to
maintain comprehensive coverage. Coverage is calculated as follows: a sub-narrative is considered
covered if at least one mapped nugget is fully supported by the response. Post-Edit Nuggets + AutoAssign. In this setting, nuggets are generated automatically using the

3.3
Support Evaluation

Support evaluation represents an attempt to determine whether information in a RAG answer is
factually supported by its cited document [8]. Evaluation in the TREC 2025 RAG is conducted by
breaking down the answer, with the unit of evaluation defined at a sentence-level (unlike response
evaluation). An answer r is assumed to be segmented into n sentences, r = {a1, · · · , an}, where
each sentence ai can contain a maximum of k document citations [d1, · · · , dk], with each document
drawn from a corpus. Support is calculated as the function f(ai, dj) where f can be a human or
LLM assessor that generates a scalar value si,j, indicating the extent to which the cited document dj
provides support for sentence ai. As with all TREC evaluations, NIST provides the resources to perform human evaluations based
on the guidance of the track organizers. NIST first trains every human assessor to understand the
task, and then each human assessor evaluates each narrative sequentially. This year, the support
evaluation focuses on sparse annotations by restricting to evaluate only the first (or highest ranked)
cited document, similar to last year [8]. For evaluation, NIST assessors use a three-level grade, with
the following descriptions:

1. Full Support: All of the information in the answer sentence is factually consistent with and
supported by the cited segment.

NIST first trains every human assessor to understand the
task, and then each human assessor evaluates each narrative sequentially. This year, the support
evaluation focuses on sparse annotations by restricting to evaluate only the first (or highest ranked)
cited document, similar to last year [8]. For evaluation, NIST assessors use a three-level grade, with
the following descriptions:

1. Full Support: All of the information in the answer sentence is factually consistent with and
supported by the cited segment.

2. Partial Support: Some of the information in the answer sentence is factually consistent with and
supported by the cited segment, but other parts of the sentence are not supported.

3. No Support: The cited segment is completely irrelevant and does not support any part of the
answer sentence. An edge case arises when a sentence contains zero citations: the support level is automatically
considered “No support”, as the sentence does not cite any retrieved segment. This year, two cases
are considered for support evaluation: Automatic, in which GPT-OSS 120B performs the automatic
assessment, and Manual, in which NIST assessors perform the evaluation and assign a support level
to each answer–document pair (Full Support, Partial Support or No Support) based on their judgment. Support is evaluated across two dimensions, following Liu et al. [2]: (1) weighted precision, which
accounts for how many correct passage citations are present in the generated answer, and (2) weighted
recall, which accounts for how many sentences in the answer are supported by passage citations. Both metrics are defined below:

Relevance Judgment Evaluation
To assess the reliability of automated labeling, the pairwise agreement, ties, and disagreements
between human judgments and participants’ labels are evaluated. This comparison helps identify
where automated methods align with expert assessments and where discrepancies occur. For runs in
which the automated labels fall within the same scale as human labels (0–4), Cohen’s Kappa score is
computed to measure the strength of agreement beyond chance.

3.4
Relevance Judgment Evaluation
To assess the reliability of automated labeling, the pairwise agreement, ties, and disagreements
between human judgments and participants’ labels are evaluated. This comparison helps identify
where automated methods align with expert assessments and where discrepancies occur. For runs in
which the automated labels fall within the same scale as human labels (0–4), Cohen’s Kappa score is
computed to measure the strength of agreement beyond chance. This metric provides a quantitative
indicator of alignment quality, ensuring that the evaluation captures both consistency and potential
gaps between human and machine judgments.

• Correlation over all narrative–run pairs (all narratives / runs). Using the same automati-
cally generated assessments as in the preceding settings, each narrative–run pair is treated as an
independent observation. Kendall’s τ is computed over the complete set of such observations. The Figure 6 presents a comparative analysis of manual evaluation scores and the outputs of the
ensemble across the and metrics. In all cases, the automated
assessments demonstrate a strong ability to approximate manual judgments at the run level, as
evidenced by the high Kendall’s τ coefficients and the close alignment of the aggregated red points
with the identity line. This indicates that, when scores are aggregated across entire runs, the automated
system captures the overall ranking behavior produced by human assessors with considerable fidelity. At finer levels of granularity, such as narrative-avg and all narratives / runs comparisons, the scatter
becomes more pronounced, suggesting greater variability in how individual narratives are evaluated
and highlighting inherent noise in localized relevance patterns. Notably, the degree of correspondence
improves at larger cutoff values, particularly for where the distribution tightens, and the
rank correlations increase. This trend suggests that the ensemble yields more stable approximations
when the metric incorporates a broader portion of the ranked list. Overall, the results indicate that the
automated method provides a reliable run-level surrogate for manual evaluation, while still exhibiting
expected deviations at the narrative level. Run ID

4.2
AG and RAG Task

For the TREC 2025 RAG Track, NIST received 51 runs from 16 groups for the RAG task and 25
runs from 9 groups for the AG task. Response Evaluation. Table 7 presents scores for AG and RAG runs with priority levels higher
than 3 under the fully manual setting (post-edited nuggets with manual assignment). Table 8 shows
scores for AG and RAG runs under the post-edited nuggets with automatic assignment using manual
relevance judgments. Table 9 shows scores for AG and RAG runs under the automated setting
evaluated using AutoNuggetizer using manual relevance judgments. Table 10 shows the fully
automated setting, which also involved automatically generated relevance judgments for document

selection. Figure 7 showcases the correlation analysis performed for the AG and RAG tasks, analogous
to the retrieval task analysis for response evaluation. The three panels in Figure 7 compare the
fully manual setting, Post-Edited Nuggets + ManualAssign, with three alternative configurations:
a fully automated pipeline using AutoNuggetizer with automatically generated qrels (top row),
AutoNuggetizer with NIST qrels (middle row), and Post-Edited Nuggets with NIST assessors with
AutoAssign (bottom row). In row (a) of Figure 7, the strict vital score exhibits the greatest degree of scatter, indicating the weakest
correlation and the lowest agreement between the fully manual and fully automated configurations. In
row (b) of Figure 7, where manually created relevance judgments are retained but nugget generation
is automated, the strict vital plots show a noticeable reduction in scatter. This improvement reflects
stronger alignment and highlights the stabilizing effect of manual relevance assessments. In row (c)
of Figure 7, which isolates the effect of nugget assignment by comparing manual and automated
assignment while keeping all other components fixed, the strict vital score exhibits the highest level
of correlation, with tightly clustered points and the strongest Kendall τ values observed across the
three comparisons. For the sub-narrative coverage metric, the pattern differs somewhat. Rows (a) and (b) of Figure 7
display comparable levels of run-level correlation, although the condition using NIST qrels shows
slightly stronger agreement, suggesting that manual relevance judgments help but exert a more limited
influence on this metric. In row (c) of Figure 7, the overall scatter of individual narrative-run points
is reduced; however, the run-level aggregated correlations decline relative to the middle row.

Rows (a) and (b) of Figure 7
display comparable levels of run-level correlation, although the condition using NIST qrels shows
slightly stronger agreement, suggesting that manual relevance judgments help but exert a more limited
influence on this metric. In row (c) of Figure 7, the overall scatter of individual narrative-run points
is reduced; however, the run-level aggregated correlations decline relative to the middle row. This
indicates that although automated assignment introduces less point-level variability, it can still alter
the ordering of runs in ways that reduce rank correlation for sub-narrative coverage. Overall, the figure
demonstrates that strict vital scores are more sensitive to upstream evaluation components, while
sub-narrative coverage remains comparatively stable except when nugget assignment is modified. Support Evaluation. Table 11 shows support evaluation scores for AG and RAG runs using
assessments performed by NIST assessors. Table 12 shows support evaluation scores for AG and
RAG runs using GPT-OSS 120B as the assessor. Figure 8 compares weighted recall and weighted
precision scores produced by GPT-OSS 120B in the automatic setting against the manual setting,
produced by NIST assessors. For both metrics, the scatterplots show a clear positive relationship,
with high run-level Kendall’s τ values indicating strong overall alignment between the model-based
and manual assessments. Although some dispersion appears at the individual narrative level, the
aggregated red points fall close to the diagonal, demonstrating that GPT-OSS 120B captures the
overall ranking structure of systems reasonably well. The similarity in patterns across both weighted
recall and weighted precision suggests that GPT-OSS 120B provides a stable approximation of human
scoring for metrics that integrate both relevance and nugget importance. Overall, Figure 8 indicates
that while fine-grained variability persists, GPT-OSS 120B serves as a reliable surrogate for manual
evaluation at the run level.

4.3
Relevance Judgment (RJ) Task

For the TREC 2025 RAG Track, NIST received 36 runs from 5 groups for the Relevance Judgment
task. Table 13 shows alignment evaluation when the participant’s submissions are compared against
human judgment. The best runs achieve agreement fractions between 0.30–0.34, suggesting that
automated relevance judgment remains a challenging task with limited agreement relative to human
assessors, even among the best-performing runs.

5
Conclusion

The TREC 2025 RAG Track represents a significant step toward advancing RAG systems capable
of handling complex, long context narratives. By shifting the focus from keyword-based queries
to nuanced, multi-sentence information needs this year, the track received over 150 submissions
that integrated deep reasoning, evidence synthesis, and transparent attribution. The multi-layered
evaluation framework ensures rigorous assessment of retrieval quality, factual grounding, and response
completeness, setting a high standard for future research. Through these innovations, the track seeks
to inspire the development of robust, trustworthy AI systems that can meet the demands of real-world
information seeking and contribute to the broader progress in IR and NLP. Acknowledgements
